title: Flaw Remediation
controlText: Identify, report, and correct information and information system flaws in a timely manner.
plainEnglish: Establish and follow a defined process for finding system flaws (vulnerabilities, misconfigurations, bugs), reporting them, and fixing them within specified timeframes. Timeframes must be documented — not just 'as soon as possible' but specific windows based on severity. This is the operational practice that makes vulnerability scanning (RA.L2-3.11.2) and remediation (RA.L2-3.11.3) actionable at the system level. The Assessment Guide's objectives cover all six steps: time to identify, actual identification, time to report, actual reporting, time to correct, and actual correction.

objectives.text: the time within which to identify system flaws is specified;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented policy or procedure specifying how frequently systems must be checked for flaws — typically via vulnerability scans on a defined schedule (monthly, quarterly). The timeframe must be written, not assumed.
commonEvidence: System and Information Integrity Policy with flaw identification timeframes
Vulnerability management procedure defining scan frequency
Patch management policy defining identification cadence

objectives.text: system flaws are identified within the specified time frame;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that identification actually occurs on schedule — vulnerability scan reports dated within the policy-defined period, configuration scan results, patch Tuesday review records.
commonEvidence: Vulnerability scan reports showing scan dates within defined period
Configuration compliance scan reports
Automated patch detection reports

objectives.text: the time within which to report system flaws is specified;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented timeframes for reporting identified flaws to the appropriate parties — security team, system owners, management. May be embedded in the vulnerability management procedure or incident response policy.
commonEvidence: Vulnerability management procedure with reporting timeframes
Escalation procedure for critical vulnerabilities

objectives.text: system flaws are reported within the specified time frame;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that reporting occurs — vulnerability scan reports distributed to security and system owners, tickets opened in the tracking system within the required timeframe, SIEM alerts for new critical CVEs.
commonEvidence: Vulnerability report distribution records
Ticketing system records showing flaw-to-ticket time
Escalation records for critical findings

objectives.text: the time within which to correct system flaws is specified; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented remediation SLAs by severity — e.g., Critical within 15 days, High within 30 days, Medium within 90 days. Must be written and specific.
commonEvidence: Patch management policy with severity-based SLAs
Vulnerability remediation SLA table
POA&M with remediation target dates

objectives.text: system flaws are corrected within the specified time frame.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that corrections happen on schedule — patch deployment records, rescan results showing vulnerabilities closed, ticket closure records with dates. Aging reports showing how long vulnerabilities have been open.
commonEvidence: Patch deployment records with deployment dates
Rescan reports confirming vulnerability closure
Vulnerability aging report showing open items vs SLA
Configuration change records addressing misconfiguration findings

commonGaps: Remediation timeframes exist in policy but are not enforced — critical vulnerabilities open for months
Flaw identification depends on manual review rather than automated scanning on a defined schedule
Patches tested and approved but never deployed to production
No rescan process — vulnerabilities marked closed without verification
Reporting step skipped — vulnerability scan runs but results are not distributed to system owners
SLAs defined for servers but not for workstations, network devices, or cloud resources

title: Malicious Code Protection
controlText: Provide protection from malicious code at appropriate locations within organizational information systems.
plainEnglish: Deploy anti-malware or endpoint protection at every location where malicious code could execute or enter: endpoints, servers, email gateways, web proxies, and removable media entry points. 'Appropriate locations' is a judgment call informed by the attack paths malicious code uses — the Assessment Guide notes that email, removable media, and websites are primary delivery vectors. The control doesn't mandate a specific product, but it does require coverage across all vectors and that the protection is actually working.

objectives.text: designated locations for malicious code protection are identified; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented identification of which system components require malicious code protection — workstations, servers, email gateway, web proxy, removable media entry points. Should be comprehensive enough to cover the attack surface.
commonEvidence: System and Information Integrity Policy listing protected locations
Endpoint protection deployment scope documentation
Email security gateway scope documentation

objectives.text: protection from malicious code at designated locations is provided.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Technical evidence that protection is deployed and active at all identified locations — EDR/AV console showing coverage across endpoints, email gateway anti-malware configuration, SIEM showing active detections. Assessors look for both coverage (all endpoints enrolled) and activity (recent detection events showing the tool is functioning).
commonEvidence: Endpoint protection management console showing deployment coverage
Email security gateway anti-malware configuration
Malware detection event logs showing active monitoring
Missing coverage report showing any gaps

commonGaps: Servers excluded from AV deployment due to performance concerns
Email gateway not scanning attachments — only endpoint AV deployed
MDM-managed mobile devices excluded from endpoint protection
Linux or macOS endpoints not covered by the Windows-centric AV deployment
AV deployed but showing stale signatures — not updating
Cloud workloads not included in endpoint protection scope

title: Security Alerts and Advisories
controlText: Monitor system security alerts and advisories and take action in response.
plainEnglish: Subscribe to security alerts and advisories from relevant sources — US-CERT/CISA, vendor security bulletins, NVD/CVE feeds, ISAC threat feeds — and act on them. The Assessment Guide requires three things: defined response actions, active monitoring, and actual response. It's not enough to be subscribed to email alerts if nobody reads them. The action response should be documented — when a critical advisory arrives, who reviews it, how applicability is assessed, and what gets done as a result (scan, patch, architecture change). The Assessment Guide example explicitly shows routing advisories through the change management process.

objectives.text: response actions to system security alerts and advisories are identified;
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented response process for security alerts — who receives them, how applicability is assessed, what response options exist (scan, patch, architecture change, accept), and who approves the response decision. Should align with the vulnerability management procedure.
commonEvidence: Security alert response procedure
Roles and responsibilities for alert review and response
Documented escalation criteria for critical advisories

objectives.text: system security alerts and advisories are monitored; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Active subscription to relevant alert sources — CISA KEV, NVD notifications, vendor security bulletins, threat intelligence feeds. Evidence that monitoring is ongoing — subscription confirmation, email distribution lists, recent alerts received and reviewed.
commonEvidence: CISA, NVD, or vendor security bulletin subscription records
Threat intelligence feed configuration
Distribution list for security advisories
Records of recent advisories received and reviewed

objectives.text: actions in response to system security alerts and advisories are taken.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that advisories result in action — change management tickets generated from advisory review, vulnerability scans triggered by new CVE disclosures, configuration changes made in response to advisories. Even a documented 'not applicable' determination with reasoning satisfies this objective.
commonEvidence: Change management tickets referencing security advisories
Vulnerability scan records triggered by advisory review
Advisory applicability assessment records
Patching records tied to specific CVE advisories

commonArtifacts: Change management tickets triggered by advisories
commonGaps: Subscribed to alert emails that nobody reads — alerts arrive but no review process
No documented process for assessing advisory applicability
Advisory review happens informally with no records — nothing to show assessors
Response limited to patching — architecture or configuration responses not considered
CISA KEV not monitored — organization unaware of actively exploited vulnerabilities
Alert review done only by one person — single point of failure with no backup coverage

title: Update Malicious Code Protection
controlText: Update malicious code protection mechanisms when new releases are available.
plainEnglish: Anti-malware signatures, definitions, and engine versions must be kept current — ideally through automatic updates on a frequency commensurate with how fast malware evolves (daily). The Assessment Guide is explicit: 'Malware changes on an hourly or daily basis, and it is important to update detection and protection mechanisms frequently.' An AV product with signatures that are two weeks old is substantially less effective than one updated daily. This applies to both the signature/definition files and the protection engine itself.

objectives.text: malicious code protection mechanisms are updated when new releases are available.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that updates are automatic and current — EDR/AV management console showing last signature update time within 24-48 hours for all enrolled endpoints. Update configuration showing automatic update enabled. Any endpoints with stale signatures should have documented justification and compensating control.
commonEvidence: EDR/AV management console showing signature update timestamps
Automatic update configuration settings
Update frequency policy specifying required update cadence
Report of endpoints with stale definitions

commonArtifacts: Endpoint protection management console showing signature currency
Definition update policy with frequency requirements
Stale definition report with any exceptions documented
commonGaps: Automatic updates disabled on servers due to change management concerns — manual update process falls behind
Endpoints in air-gapped or isolated segments not receiving definition updates
Update verification not monitored — signatures drift out of date without anyone noticing
Mobile devices excluded from centrally-managed signature updates
Engine updates (not just definition updates) deferred indefinitely due to compatibility concerns

title: System and File Scanning
controlText: Perform periodic scans of the information system and real-time scans of files from external sources as files are downloaded, opened, or executed.
plainEnglish: Two types of scanning are required: periodic scans (scheduled full-system or disk scans against current signatures, typically daily or weekly) and real-time scanning of files arriving from external sources — downloads, email attachments, removable media, and any file opened from an external location. Real-time scanning is the primary protection against drive-by downloads and weaponized attachments. The Assessment Guide example explicitly mentions email gateway scanning and removable media scanning as required coverage areas.

objectives.text: the frequency for malicious code scans is defined;
objectives.whatToLookFor: A documented policy or procedure specifying how often malicious code (periodic) scans must be performed — typically daily or weekly. The frequency must be written, not assumed. Look for the System and Information Integrity Policy, malicious code protection procedure, or endpoint protection configuration baseline that states the scan schedule.
commonEvidence: System and Information Integrity Policy with malicious code scan frequency
Endpoint protection configuration baseline specifying scan schedule
Malicious code protection procedure defining scan cadence

objectives.text: malicious code scans are performed with the defined frequency; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Evidence that periodic malicious code scans are actually running on the defined schedule. Scheduled scan configuration enabled on endpoints and servers. Scan completion logs showing scans are executing on time. Management console showing last scan completion date within the required period.
commonEvidence: Endpoint protection scheduled scan configuration
Scan completion logs from the management console
Periodic scan result summaries with dates

objectives.text: real-time malicious code scans of files from external sources as files are downloaded, opened, or executed are performed.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Real-time protection configuration enabled on all endpoints — on-access scanning active, email attachment scanning at the gateway or endpoint, removable media scanning triggered on connection. Configuration evidence plus detection event records showing real-time scanning is functioning.
commonEvidence: Real-time/on-access scanning configuration
Email gateway attachment scanning configuration
Removable media scan policy and configuration
Real-time detection event logs

commonArtifacts: Periodic scan schedule and completion records
commonGaps: Periodic scans scheduled but excluded large directories or drives — significant coverage gaps
Real-time scanning disabled on high-performance servers due to resource impact
Email attachment scanning at gateway only — no endpoint real-time scanning for files downloaded via browser
Removable media not included in real-time scan scope
Scan logs not retained — no evidence of scan activity available for review
On-access scanning bypassed by users with local admin rights

title: Monitor Communications for Attacks
controlText: Monitor organizational systems, including inbound and outbound communications traffic, to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks.
plainEnglish: Deploy monitoring at the network boundary and within the environment to detect attacks and indicators of attack in both inbound and outbound traffic. The Assessment Guide explicitly requires monitoring of both directions — inbound for external attacks, outbound for signs of compromise (malware beaconing, unauthorized exfiltration, lateral movement signaling). Tools include IDS/IPS, network traffic analysis, SIEM with network event sources, DNS monitoring, and proxy logs. The Assessment Guide notes that output from system monitoring 'serves as input to continuous monitoring and incident response programs,' directly linking this practice to CA.L2-3.12.3 and IR.L2-3.6.1.

objectives.text: the system is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks;
objectives.whatToLookFor: System-level monitoring deployed — EDR behavioral detection on endpoints, host-based IDS/IPS, SIEM with system event correlation. Evidence that system monitoring is producing output that analysts review: SIEM alerts, EDR alerts, detection event logs.
commonEvidence: EDR behavioral detection configuration
SIEM event correlation rules for attack indicators
Host-based IDS/IPS configuration
System monitoring event logs and alert records

objectives.text: inbound communications traffic is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Network monitoring covering inbound traffic — IDS/IPS at the perimeter, firewall log analysis, web application firewall if applicable, DNS sinkhole or monitoring. Detection rules covering known attack signatures and anomalous inbound patterns. SIEM ingesting network boundary logs.
commonEvidence: Network IDS/IPS configuration showing inbound coverage
Perimeter firewall log analysis configuration
Web application firewall configuration if applicable
DNS monitoring configuration
SIEM ingestion from perimeter devices

objectives.text: outbound communications traffic is monitored to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Outbound monitoring capability — web proxy logs, DNS query monitoring, DLP or CASB for data exfiltration detection, firewall outbound log analysis, SIEM rules for unusual outbound patterns (beaconing, large transfers, connections to known-malicious IPs/domains).
commonEvidence: Web proxy or secure web gateway configuration
Outbound firewall log analysis rules in SIEM
DNS monitoring for C2 indicators
DLP or CASB outbound monitoring configuration
Threat intelligence feed integration for outbound IOC matching

commonArtifacts: SIEM configuration showing network data sources
Web proxy / secure web gateway configuration
Monitoring coverage documentation (what is monitored, where sensors are placed)
commonGaps: Inbound monitoring only — no outbound traffic analysis, missing exfiltration and C2 activity
IDS/IPS deployed at perimeter but not for internal east-west traffic
SIEM collects logs but no detection rules configured for network attack patterns
DNS not monitored — major gap for detecting malware C2 and DNS tunneling
EDR deployed but behavioral detection disabled — only signature detection active
No DLP or outbound content inspection — unauthorized data exfiltration not detectable

title: Identify Unauthorized Use
controlText: Identify unauthorized use of organizational systems.
plainEnglish: Define what authorized use looks like — documented in an acceptable use policy, by role, by system — and then actively monitor for deviations. The Assessment Guide explicitly requires both definition (what is authorized) and detection (identifying what is not authorized). The practice is explicitly linked to AC.L1-3.1.1 (authorized users), AU.L2-3.3.1 (audit logs), and IA.L1-3.5.1/3.5.2 (identification) — those controls establish who can access what; this control detects when someone does something they shouldn't. Tools include SIEM user behavior analytics, EDR, DLP, UEBA, privileged access monitoring, and log review processes.

objectives.text: authorized use of the system is defined; and
objectives.whatToLookFor: Documented definition of what constitutes authorized use — acceptable use policy covering data types permitted for storage/processing, authorized personnel, permitted time windows, authorized software, and authorized external connections. Should be specific enough to make unauthorized use detectable.
commonEvidence: Acceptable Use Policy
System-specific authorized use documentation
User account provisioning policy defining permitted access scope
Authorized software list

objectives.text: unauthorized use of the system is identified.
objectives.whatToLookFor: Active monitoring capability that can detect when authorized use boundaries are violated — SIEM user behavior rules, EDR process monitoring, DLP detection of data handling violations, privileged access monitoring, UEBA if deployed. Evidence that unauthorized use detections actually occur and are investigated.
commonEvidence: SIEM user behavior analysis rules
EDR process and file access monitoring configuration
DLP policy violation detection logs
Privileged access monitoring records
Records of investigated unauthorized use incidents
UEBA platform configuration if deployed

commonArtifacts: DLP policy configuration and violation records
commonGaps: Acceptable use policy exists but no technical monitoring enforces or detects violations
Authorized use defined at a high level — insufficient specificity to make unauthorized use detectable
SIEM deployed but no user behavior detection rules configured
Privileged user actions not monitored — admins can do anything without detection
No investigation process for potential unauthorized use alerts — alerts fire but nobody reviews them
Assessment Guide explicitly links this to AC.L1-3.1.1 and AU.L2-3.3.1 — if those controls are weak, this one fails too
